# A delay seen through censoring and truncation

## How do we currently suggest people estimate and report a delay distribution?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="38%"}
- Adjust for **within-interval censoring of both events** (always)
- **Right truncation** is the correction for dynamical
  (epidemic-phase) bias
- Fit **several distributions** and compare with model-fit statistics
- Report uncertainty, and share de-identified line lists and code

::: {.callout-note}
Later, the Andes-virus analysis works through a version of this
checklist:
[andv-linelist-analysis](https://epiforecasts.io/andv-linelist-analysis/dev/charniga).
:::

::: {.attribution}
Flowchart: Fig 3 of
[Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520),
PLOS Comput Biol, CC-BY 4.0
:::
:::
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](figures/fig-charniga-flowchart.png){height="900px"}
:::
::::

## One delay, built up in three steps

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- For now treat the serial interval as a single **event-to-event**
  delay, ignoring that it is itself composed — we return to that later
- Daily or weekly data put both the primary (infector) and secondary
  (infectee) events in **windows**, not at points
- In real time, long delays have not happened yet
- Build the observation model up in three steps:
  1. primary interval censoring
  2. secondary interval censoring
  3. right truncation
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-double-censoring.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk
:::
:::
::::

## 1. Primary interval censoring

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- The infector's event falls in a window $[0, w_P]$
- It need not be uniform, but a uniform primary is a fair assumption,
  especially when the window is short
- Mix the delay over where the event sat in the window — a convolution
  on the CDF
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-primary-censoring.png){width="100%"}

$$
F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(t) = \int_{0}^{w_P} g(\tau)\, F(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

::: {.attribution}
Maths after
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
We can solve this integral, and have done so analytically for common
families: gamma, lognormal, and Weibull with a uniform primary window.
:::

## 2. Secondary interval censoring

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- The infectee's onset is also recorded to the day
- The probability in a daily bin is a difference of the
  primary-censored CDF
- This doubles as a **probability mass function** for a continuous
  delay in daily discrete-time models (`EpiNow2`, `EpiEstim`,
  `epinowcast`)
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-secondary-censoring.png){width="100%"}

$$
\Pr\!\big(T \in [n, n + w_S]\big)
  = F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(n + w_S) - F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(n)
$$

::: {.attribution}
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
[CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
In the past this was often written as a double integral, which is
typically more complicated to evaluate.
:::

## 3. Right truncation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- In real time we only see a pair if the secondary event is before the
  cutoff $C$
- Condition on being observed: divide by the CDF at the truncation
  horizon
- Short delays are over-represented early in an epidemic
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-truncation.png){width="100%"}

$$
L^{\mathrm{RT}}
  = \frac{F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(n + w_S) - F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(n)}
         {F_{\mathrm{PEC}}(C - P)}
$$

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
Each step is a different bias adjustment, composed together.
:::
